data.title: General Mills Has Seen Excessive Gloom And Doom
data.description:
In late October, I upgraded General Mills, Inc. (GIS) to a buy rating. I expressed my belief that better days were ahead, as there were some signs of stabilization and the valuation was near multiyear lows. Since then, the stock has continued to slump, as it's now down around 9% since the publication of my update. The company reported their most recent results mid last month, and so today, we'll see if I have indeed jumped the gun with my upgrade.

Below, it is shown that Q2 was painful but a decent quarter for the company. Organic sales declines have slowed, and they were able to beat top and bottom line expectations. The company also laid out their priorities for FY2026 that make sense, and if they are able to execute, it seems the long term outlook is solid. General Mills failed to deliver a beat and raise, but full-year guidance still shows that the worst is likely over. Especially when considering that the valuation remains at rock-bottom levels, I still see brighter days ahead for the company and for the stock. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating.

Q2 Sales Analysis

For FY2026 Q2, General Mills posted rather worrying top-line results. They had net sales of $4.861 billion, which represents a decline of 7% YoY. As you can see in the chart above, the overall downtrend in growth doesn't seem to have ended, and in fact, Q2 represented the worst sales decline in the past few years. Therefore, it seems that their business is seeing a worsening trajectory. Still, General Mills beat analyst expectations by $78.14 million, and so results were better than feared.

While reported declines worsened, as a potentially encouraging sign, their organic net sales actually only decreased by 1% YoY. That is an improvement from the previous quarter's 3% decline. Since organic sales are usually a better reflection of a company's core business activity, I would say that there are actually some encouraging signs that aren't shown in the reported GAAP figure. As you can see in the breakdown of sales growth above, acquisitions & divestitures were the key contributor to the reported sales weakness. In organic terms, there was some mild weakness in price/mix, perhaps as a result of consumers choosing cheaper goods, but volume held steady as a sign of stability and resilience.

Before moving on, it is worth mentioning there was some notable difference between their North America sales overall and international sales. In North America, their best performing segment by organic net sales growth was Pet at 1%, while the worst was their Retail at -3%. For their international segment, they saw 4% of organic growth, and so there is quite a contrast. In my view, this continues to reflect relative consumer weakness in the U.S. as a result of sticky inflation near 3% as well as volatile tariff policy from the Trump Administration. The underperformance in their domestic market is clearly a drag on performance and that is potentially a concern since the North American market makes up the vast majority of their sales.

Pain In Their Margins

Overall, there is significant pain seen in the company's margins. For adjusted gross margin, they saw 150 basis points of contraction, and they mainly attributed this to higher input costs. When companies like General Mills state that there were higher input costs, it more than likely means that tariffs have eroded the company's cost control. Therefore, it really is a double whammy that tariffs are both hitting consumer confidence and cost control measures. This leads to a significant erosion of profitability.

For adjusted operating margin, things are also a bit worrying. For Q2, they saw 290 basis points of contraction YoY. They attributed this to higher SG&A expenses as a percentage of net sales, and so there are signs that corporate efficiency is also weaker than desirable.

At the end of the day, General Mills reported an adjusted EPS of $1.10. While EPS declines accelerated from 19.6% in Q1 to 21.4%, note that they were able to still beat expectations by a respectable $0.07. Therefore, even though profitability is definitely shaky, Q2 was still arguably an outperforming quarter for the bottom line.

Strategy Is Reasonable

General Mills laid out their FY2026 priorities, and I would say that they make sense considering where they are right now. The first priority, returning North America Retail to volume growth is obvious crucial. This segment is their largest by a considerable margin, and so they would be wise to heavily invest in this area to boost performance over time.

Their second priority, to accelerate North America Pet growth, is also a very intuitive decision. As stated earlier, this segment was their strongest by organic net sales growth in North America, and so it makes sense that they double down here and ride some of the momentum they have been seeing.

Lastly, for their third priority, they are focused on driving efficiencies. After the margin discussion above, I believe that this is obviously an important area to focus on. Overall, I would say that General Mills has laid out their strategy quite nicely. Now, it's likely a matter of execution, and that's equally as important.

Capital Returns Suggest Cautiousness

While Seeking Alpha rates their dividend very favorably, as shown on the right, there are some signs that the company is a bit cautious with their capital returns as a whole. In Q2, General Mills basically didn't buy back any stock, while in the year ago quarter, they bought back $300 million. For dividends, their total payout dropped from $338 million to $327.9 million. While that's probably just because of a decrease in the number of shares rather than a cut in the dividend, I would say that overall, the company seems a bit hesitant to distribute capital in this tough macro environment. The company may also be using capital to fund their strategy that I discussed above, and so it's not that big of a surprise that they have adjusted their capital return policy accordingly.

Outlook Reiterated

Despite beating on both the top and bottom line in Q2, General Mills left their FY2026 guidance unchanged, and so there may be a bit of disappointment. In my previous article, I already discussed the figures shown in the chart above and concluded that guidance is a bit shaky, but the worst could also be over. Here, I'll just briefly highlight some points.

Back in FY2025, they had organic net sales growth of -2%, and so FY2026 is setting up to be a mild improvement. While the above ranges for adjusted operating profit growth and adjusted EPS growth represent deteriorations from the previous year, as stated in their guidance above, there are notable impacts from divestitures & acquisitions as well as from normalization of corporate incentive expense. When excluding these headwinds and effects, declines may actually be less than in FY2025. Lastly, note that both organic net sales and adjusted EPS guidance were above consensus expectations despite being unchanged.

Valuation At Rock Bottom Levels

I concluded that General Mills' valuation was cheap in my previous update when the forward P/E ratio was at 13.41. Since then, there has been further contraction to the current reading of 12.16. I believe that despite the substantial amount of pain the company is going through right now, the valuation does offer a notable amount of opportunity.

Firstly, in Q2, organic sales declines slowed as a promising sign, and despite the margin contraction, they still beat expectations on the bottom line. Looking forward, the company has laid out a clear and sensible strategy in my view and if they are able to execute, I would say that they are positioned relatively well. Furthermore, while they left full year guidance unchanged, FY2026 is still shaping up to be a better year for organic sales growth and potentially for adjusted EPS growth as well when excluding the headwinds stated above.

Therefore, not much has changed with regard to my view that brighter days are ahead. With that in mind, I believe the extremely depressed valuation indicates that there is considerable opportunity at the stock's current levels.

Recap

There is no question that the company is currently in a state of pain due to the macro environment and tariff headwinds. That said, I would say that there has been too much gloom and doom. As mentioned above, I stated that better days are ahead in my previous update. Today, I found Q2 results that were decent considering the state of things and FY2026 guidance that was reiterated. Therefore, I see no reason for the valuation to have contracted further since October. In my view, the story for General Mills and its stock has remained unchanged, and so I'm reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Fed Holds Steady And Signals Longer-Term Easing Bias
data.description:
Amid a rush of domestic and foreign policy developments that have rattled markets – including the potential for another U.S. government shutdown – the Federal Reserve held steady on U.S. monetary policy.

At the January meeting, the Fed held interest rates steady in a range of 3.5%–3.75% after cutting by 75 basis points over the three previous meetings. Despite the hold, the forward guidance language in the Fed’s policy statement suggests most officials agree that further easing will eventually be appropriate, although there is no sense of urgency. We expect the Fed will remain on hold until later this year, when rate cuts are likely to resume.

Risks diminishing on both sides of the Fed’s mandate

Ahead of the meeting, public remarks by Fed officials suggested that previously divergent views on inflation and labor market risks have converged somewhat.

Recent data point to solid economic momentum, improved labor market stability, and relaxing inflationary pressures. This has helped lower the risks to both sides of the Fed’s mandate (maximum employment and price stability) and thus supported the hold-steady approach.

At the press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell didn’t appear in a hurry to cut interest rates. He emphasized that rates are now closer to neutral, while data have strengthened the U.S. growth outlook and reduced downside labor market risk. He emphasized that monetary policy is in a good place to react to incoming developments.

The one surprise of January’s meeting was Governor Christopher Waller dissenting in favor of a quarter-point rate cut, along with Governor Stephen Miran (whose dissent was expected).

Waller has been advocating for rate cuts in light of what he describes as risks to labor markets along with moderating inflation once tariff effects are excluded. However, his public comments ahead of the January meeting did not appear to make a strong case for another rate cut this soon.

U.S. inflation set to moderate despite firm GDP growth

Our outlook for further rate cuts later this year is based in part on inflation trends. Although core inflation is running around 3% now (versus the Fed’s target of 2%), several forces could help lower inflation even if real GDP growth remains firm.

AI implementation and investment, which accelerated in 2025, have coincided with (and helped drive) higher productivity growth and moderating unit labor cost inflation.

In time, this should help tame price inflation. Furthermore, tariff-related price increases also appear to have mostly run their course. If inflation data continues to ease, the Fed is likely to gradually ease monetary policy as well.

Broader macroeconomic trends inform our U.S. inflation outlook. In our latest Cyclical Outlook, “Compounding Opportunity,” we discuss how U.S. policy pivots and tariff-related costs prompted a surge in AI adoption and investment as companies aggressively defended margins.

Resilient aggregate growth has masked winners and losers, and labor markets have been a key loser. Indeed, as real labor income growth has slowed, the labor market, rather than consumer prices, has realized a greater-than-expected portion of the tariff-related adjustment. This has two longer-term implications for consumer price inflation.

First, although estimates suggest that only around 50% of total tariff costs have been passed on to U.S. consumer goods prices, we think these tariff-related price hikes have largely run their course as the downward adjustment in unit labor costs helped make up the difference for companies. This means we are probably at or close to peak goods inflation.

Second, AI-related trends appear to be reducing labor’s share of income. In the third quarter, it fell to the lowest level in the history of recorded data, which spans almost 80 years.

We expect continued AI implementation in 2026 (implementation actually remains fairly low in absolute terms), which will likely keep productivity outpacing nominal wage growth – while unit labor cost inflation sinks below levels consistent with the Fed’s target, and helps moderate price inflation over time.

U.S. housing market dynamics also support disinflation. Slowing household formation (possibly due in part to changes in immigration policy and related demographic trends) coupled with greater multifamily housing supply, especially in the Sun Belt cities, is softening rental demand while housing supply expands.

This has reduced nationwide new lease rental inflation below owners’ equivalent rent (OER) for the past year, after it materially outpaced average rents in the wake of the pandemic.

New lease rents are now more aligned with the broader measure of OER, which reflects average rents for both new leases and renewals across the U.S. economy. Without more need for average rents to catch up, OER inflation is now poised to undershoot pre-pandemic levels as market rental inflation remains subdued.

Key takeaway: Steady Fed awaits clear signals in the data before it resumes easing

January’s meeting reinforces a familiar Fed narrative: The central bank collectively sees policy as restrictive enough to cool inflation, and flexible enough to absorb shocks as officials wait for clearer confirmation before easing further.

With inflation pressures broadly receding – helped by productivity gains, fading tariff effects, and softer housing costs – the central bank is likely to stay on hold for now and resume rate cuts later this year. Unless growth weakens materially or inflation reaccelerates, the next phase of policy is likely to be gradual, measured easing rather than a rapid pivot.

Disclosures

All Investments contain risk and may lose value.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their situation. This material contains the opinions of the author and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

data.title: BUI's Comeback Setup: Income Today, Tailwinds Tomorrow
data.description:
The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) is an active hybrid portfolio, combining defensive utilities exposure with infra and power themes. Although the hybrid methodology implies greater growth than utilities and lower volatility than energy/infra, performance in the recent past has been lackluster on both counts. This is attributable primarily to BUI's currency exposures in a strong USD environment, among other factors.

I expect this underperformance to change in 2026 as the USD consolidates or weakens, providing relative tailwinds in an environment where utilities, infra and power sectors are likely to see decent growth. The option layer could add income (although BUI does not aggressively use this layer). BUI's income strength also adds to its Buy appeal.

The Proposition - Enhanced Utilities

BUI is an active portfolio that should be imagined like an enhanced utilities strategy. The typical utilities portfolio provides defensive equity exposure, focusing on dividends while keeping volatility low. BUI uses this profile as a base and adds a decent amount of infrastructure and power bets. Sectoral exposure beyond utilities adds higher growth potential, inflation pass-through and better performance in reflationary cycles. But it also introduces cyclicality and greater political and regulatory risks. That makes the combination more real-asset-like and less bond-like than utilities alone.

The current sector mix supports that enhanced utilities positioning well. Utilities are still more than half of the portfolio, retaining the defensive and income oriented core. However, the ~14% in energy and ~13% in capital goods signals meaningful exposure to power generation, grid equipment, and infrastructure buildout rather than just regulated rate-based businesses. The transportation sleeve (~9%) adds inflation-linked assets like rail, toll roads, and logistics players. However, the balance ensures the drift into cyclicals is not significant.

The BUI portfolio is also meaningfully diversified outside the US (around ~40% of the portfolio, and ~30% ex-US/Canada). This lowers sensitivity to the US rate regime, diversifies regulatory risks, and also capitalizes on Canada and Europe's renewables and electrification related energy transformation. BUI is therefore far removed from being just a US bond substitute to a diversified, income-oriented real assets fund. The large cap focus (~92% large caps in the portfolio) keeps the hunt for growth and reflationary stability from turning speculative and retains the utilities' defensive traits to a large extent. It also makes it easier to write options on (more predictable share price movements) - something BUI does to enhance income, although moderately. The covered call layer accounts for less than 10% of the overall portfolio by value. This is not aggressive call writing; it ensures upside capture is intact and only minor but more reliable income is added. Also, I don't expect infrastructure and utility stocks to generate a lot of premiums, so the income enhancement is not likely to be eye-catching, but consistent and reliable.

BUI's overall structure is therefore geared for income first, smoother returns next, and greater upsides last. Investors seeking income (without MLP/K-1 headaches) without heightened credit risks should find BUI's positioning valuable. Cash yields will be higher than utility vehicles, while the volatility is dampened compared to infra ETFs.

The top 10 holdings reinforce BUI's positioning as an enhanced utilities play rather than a pure defensive fund. Core regulated large caps like NextEra, Duke, Southern, AEP, and National Grid anchor cash flow stability, while names such as TC Energy, Williams, and Cheniere add midstream and LNG exposure tied to global energy plays. Union Pacific introduces inflation-linked transport infrastructure, and Aena adds regulated airport infrastructure. The mix shows a tilt toward large, systemically important operators where earnings are asset-backed but still benefit from electrification, energy security, and infrastructure investment themes.

Performance Shows Cracks

So investors who want to enhance income, boost total returns over utilities, and limit drawdowns below infra enter BUI, and this is what they see. Over the past 5 years, BUI has underperformed even pure utility strategies in terms of total returns. And drawdowns were sharper than pure infra plays. BUI's income promise is the only parameter that has lived up to the proposition. At $0.14 dividends monthly, the yield translates to over 6% annually - a far superior income tool compared to IDU's 2-3% yield range.

The disappointment with respect to drawdowns and total returns holds crucial clues that help extrapolate BUI's forward expectations. The first drag in BUI is because of the option layer. Currently it hovers around 10%, and while this could be dynamically and actively moved up or down, the odds of lower coverage look higher judging by how the drawdown cushion is missing in 2022-23. A well covered portfolio should at least see limited but some outperformance in drawdowns through a higher amount of premiums received. So, while the covered call layer could be capping BUI's total returns, it should not be the most dominant drag. For context, if the broader utilities strategies rallied by ~55% (close to real total returns over the last 5 years) and 10% of BUI's portfolio completely returned zero because of the option cap, the remaining 90% should have still captured 90% of the ~55% rally, i.e. returned closer to 50% than the ~46% returns we see today. And this is extremely conservative math I have illustrated for context.

The fact that the utilities dominant stance drags BUI's performance compared to infra strategies' marginally higher returns is understandable, but in the last 5 years there is little outperformance in infra too. So, that sectoral tilt also does not explain the underperformance. Geographic drag could be a factor, particularly given USD was strong in 2022-25.

Inefficient FX hedging alone could be a factor explaining the performance drag. BUI has ~35% exposure to foreign currencies. BUI's currency exposure operates at two levels. Because of global exposure, some holdings are listed in non-US markets and report in local currencies, so their share prices and dividends have to be translated to USD at the fund level. More importantly, many of these non-US utilities and infra companies generate a majority of their revenues outside the US. The tariffs, costs, and allowed returns are set in local currencies. Even stable local currency earnings translate into weaker USD results when the dollar strengthens. For example, holdings like National Grid (U.K.), Aena (Spain), and TC Energy (Canada) earn much of their regulated or contracted cash flow in pounds, euros, and Canadian dollars. When the U.S. dollar strengthens, those otherwise stable local earnings and dividends translate into fewer dollars at the fund level, creating a performance headwind.

Extrapolating Ahead

From the portfolio structure to actual performance outcomes, it is clear what BUI needs to outperform. It needs an environment where utilities continue to do well. That generally translates to low or stable rates. It definitely needs a weakening USD. A weakening dollar expectation is likely to work out in 2026, with growth slowing in the near term and labor market uncertainties pressuring the USD. Usually, lower rates compared to the rest of the world are a drag for the USD too.

As the USD weakens (or consolidates), BUI's portfolio will gain a relative advantage in 2026. Lower rates are also supportive for utilities in general too. So, overall, I expect BUI's gap with pure-play utilities or infra strategies to diminish in 2026.

The gap between infrastructure's growth expectations and utilities' defensive characteristics wasn't seen much in the last cycle. With rates easing and utilities getting a valuation boost (plus expansion in a predictable low-rate environment), the convergence between infra and utilities is likely to remain. So the infra and utilities diversification is unlikely to show up as near-term benefits in BUI. However, both sectors will likely be in a supportive environment that should drive decent total returns. In other words, BUI should do relatively well (at least close the gap versus pure play utilities or infra) and absolutely well because the sectoral tilt should ensure a decent yield and total returns. This is despite the diversification benefits still not showing up.

What finally tilts BUI to a Buy is that while delivering similar total returns, it should continue to deliver much higher income. This is somewhat enabled by an option layer that enhances the income aspect, particularly in flat or weaker markets. This is not a huge enhancer given weak coverage, but it should add precious basis points.

Therefore, despite lackluster performance in the recent past, BUI qualifies as a Buy on forward expectations. I would certainly position it as a satellite allocation in income portfolios primarily driven by its higher yields, in an expected regime of relative tailwinds that should at least ensure total returns similar to IDU or IGF.

data.title: Constellation Software: One Of The Last Places AI Entrepreneurs Will Target
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Giverny Capital Asset Management Q4 2025 Letter.

The fifth member of the laggards in 2025 was Constellation Software (CSU), another business that we have owned since our inception and that had been among our best stocks for most of that time. Constellation declined 22% for the year, even though I believe earnings will rise by nearly 20% for the full year. [The company reports full year results in early February]. The stock continued its steep decline in the first weeks of 2026.

Software businesses are perceived as facing an existential threat from AI models that can write software faster than humans can. AI-written software cannot yet replace existing products, but the market is looking ahead and saying: that day is coming.

In response, I'll argue that Constellation is a particular flavor of software company. It owns hundreds of small firms that have created bespoke products that customers use to run their operations. Scores of golf courses around the country run on various permutations of Constellation products, as do public transit systems, truck fleets, food service operations, hospitals and medical offices, and many others. No one product or customer represents a hefty amount of revenue. Each customer tends to have a unique operating system, even when they are in a similar enterprise. The software is not necessarily complex, but it is specific to its user. Most users also depend on Constellation for support, as a golf course or city bus system typically lacks sophisticated tech resources in house.

In sum, Constellation strikes me as one of the last places AI entrepreneurs will target. You'd be fighting for small pots of revenue with high customization and support needs. To date, AI software has proven buggy and not good at self-repair, in part because the AI models don't train on mistakes, so they don't know how to respond to them. AI software can also hallucinate and cause errors that people must then find and fix.

Nevertheless, investors have abandoned Constellation over the past six months. It further hurt that the brilliant founder-CEO Mark Leonard retired abruptly in September for health reasons. Constellation now trades at the cheapest price relative to earnings that I can recall. We're very much living in a momentum-based market environment, but if Constellation's earnings keep growing 15% to 20%, the stock price eventually will reflect that. For now, software businesses are radioactive.

Disclosures

The performance returns presented herein are those of a Poppe family account (the "Portfolio") that serves as the model portfolio of Giverny Capital Asset Management LLC ("GCAM"). The Portfolio is managed in accordance with the investment strategy that GCAM employs for its client accounts; however, the performance of a client account may differ from that of the Portfolio due to account size, client-specific guidelines or restrictions, tax considerations, cash flows into and out of the account and timing of transactions and other factors. Performance returns of the Portfolio as of the most recent quarter end can be found at www. givernycam. com/performance.

The views expressed herein are those of GCAM as of the date of this letter and are subject to change without notice.

GCAM makes no representations or warranties regarding the completeness or accuracy of any information contained herein, and does not guarantee that any forecast, projection or opinion will be realized. This letter is presented for informational purposes only, and the information herein is not intended, and should not be construed, as investment advice or as an offer or recommendation to buy or sell any security.

All investments involve risk and may lose value. For a discussion of risks, see Item 8 of GCAM's Form ADV Brochure.

Certain statements herein are "forward looking statements. "

Forward-looking statements are subject to risks, uncertainties and other factors that could cause actual results to differ materially from future results expressed or implied by such forward-looking statements. Readers should carefully consider such factors. Forward-looking statements speak only as of the date on which such statements are made; GCAM undertakes no obligation to update any forward-looking statements to reflect events or circumstances after the date of such statements.
